title: Striation-Overlay Comparator
description: Analyze toolmark striations the way a comparison microscope does. Two horizontal strips are stacked: the crime-scene toolmark on top and a suspect tool's test mark below, each a set of parallel vertical striation lines. Pick a suspect tool, slide its test-mark strip to align its striations with the scene mark, then score the alignment as a correlation percentage. Exactly one of the three tools shares the scene's individual striation pattern; the class-width readout also lets a mismatched tool be excluded on class alone.
creator: Forensic Science Intelligent Textbook
subject: Forensic Science
Firearms and Ballistics
Toolmark Examination
Striation Comparison
Comparison Microscope
bloomLevel: Analyze (L4) — compare two toolmark striation patterns by aligning them and scoring their correlation to identify or exclude a suspect tool.

educational.topic: Aligning and scoring toolmark striations to identify or exclude a suspect tool
learningObjectives: Distinguish class characteristics (mark width) from individual characteristics (striation pattern).
Align a suspect tool's striations to a crime-scene toolmark as a comparison microscope does.
Score striation correlation and interpret a match versus no-match verdict.
Exclude a tool on class characteristics and identify the source tool on individual characteristics.
educational.prerequisites: Familiarity with toolmarks, striations, and the difference between class and individual characteristics

pedagogical.interactionPattern: The crime-scene toolmark is drawn as a strip of vertical striation lines at the top; the selected suspect tool's test mark is a second strip below it, separated by a comparison-microscope bridge line. A dropdown chooses among three suspect tools; a slider shifts the suspect strip horizontally to align its striations with the scene mark. 'Score Alignment' computes the percentage of scene striations that coincide with a suspect striation within tolerance at the current shift, prints a MATCH or NO MATCH verdict, and draws green vertical guides through the coinciding lines. 'Reset' returns the shift to zero. A class-width readout marks a tool as excluded on class when its width differs from the scene mark.
pedagogical.assessment: Analysis with immediate feedback: the student first checks the class width to see whether a tool can be excluded outright, then slides the striations into alignment and scores the individual match. Because only one tool's striation pattern truly aligns, a wrong tool never reaches the match threshold no matter how it is shifted, reinforcing that a real identification survives small alignment adjustments while a coincidental one does not.
pedagogical.modelType: Translation-correlation model: each strip is a set of striation positions given as fractions of strip width. The suspect strip has a fixed offset plus a user shift; two striations coincide when their pixel positions fall within a fixed tolerance. The correlation score is the fraction of scene striations with a coinciding suspect striation. Only the matching tool shares the scene's positions, so it reaches 100% at the aligning shift.
controls: 'Tool' dropdown — choose Tool 1 (Screwdriver), Tool 2 (Chisel), or Tool 3 (Bolt cutter)
'Shift (align)' slider — translate the suspect strip horizontally to align striations
'Score Alignment' button — compute the correlation percentage and show the verdict
'Reset' button — return the shift to zero and clear the verdict
